question: Identify the root cause of the network fault: which device, what changed, and why it breaks.
description:
R1 grows an orphan `area 2` block with no interface bindings —
configuration parity with several brownfield reference designs that
carry dead OSPF lines from copy-paste. The block has no operational
effect on adjacencies or routing (no interface, no `network`
statement), but it shows up in `show running-config router ospf`
output and can mislead config-diff tools and human reviewers. Useful
for testing the RCA agent's ability to distinguish "this stanza is
meaningless" from "this stanza is the bug."
expected_rca:
No operational root cause: the change is inert.
On 100.64.0.101 (R1) an empty OSPF `area 2` block was added with no interface and no network binding, so it has zero effect on any adjacency or route - it only appears in the running-config and can mislead config-diff tools.
The correct conclusion is that there is no real fault.
expected_symptoms_summary:
BGP session state change (%ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE):
- R2: neighbor 192.0.2.3.
- R3: neighbor 192.0.2.2.
- R3: neighbor 192.0.2.6.

%ROUTING-BGP-5-NSR_STATE_CHANGE reported by R6 (1 instance):
- R6: Changed state to Not NSR-Ready

revert.commands:
configure terminal
router ospf 100
no area 2

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:40:19.198 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.2 Up (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:40:23.625 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.6 Up (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:40:23.625 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-NSR_STATE_CHANGE : Changed state to Not NSR-Ready
